metadata.title: Ship It: Figma Link + Screen-Grab Submission
metadata.playlist: Plan It Like a PM — Your Madison PRD
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 2 brief — Plan Your Madison Project Like a Pro

beats.narration_text: E7. Two submissions, specific rules, easy points to lose. Don't lose them. You submit two things: a Figma link with comments enabled and a screen grab of the whole board. The file has a required name. There's a deadline conflict in the assignment brief that you need to confirm with your instructor. And the rubric has four deductions worth 35 points combined — all avoidable. This episode covers each requirement exactly.

output: Submit TWO things: Figma link + screen grab
Canvas submission · confirm the real deadline with your instructor

beats.narration_text: Before you export anything, do the final board pass. Five checks. One: every section is labeled with the section name and its point value — for example, 'Part 1 — Dream Job (10 pts).' If a reviewer can't immediately see what a section is worth, that's a presentation problem. Two: the board style is consistent. Font, color, and spacing shouldn't drift between sections you added in different episodes — do one pass now looking only at visual consistency. Three: comments are enabled. In Figma, click Share, then confirm that 'Anyone with the link can view and comment' is selected. Comment access is a stated requirement in the assignment brief. Four: the link is publicly accessible — paste it into an incognito window right now and confirm the full board loads without you being logged in. Five: scope check — is any section proposing something unrealistic for a single week? The deduction for unrealistic scope is ten points, the second-largest on the rubric. Catch it now, before submission.

artifactLines: 1. Every section labeled with name + point value (e.g., 'Part 1 — Dream Job (10 pts)')
2. Board style consistent — font, color, spacing don't drift between sections
3. Comments enabled — Share → 'Anyone with the link can view and comment'
4. Link publicly accessible — test in an incognito window before submitting
5. Scope check — nothing on the board that couldn't be built in one week  (-10 deduction risk)
shot.sparkLine: label → style → comments → incognito test → scope

beats.narration_text: Now export the screen grab. In Figma, select all frames — Command A on Mac, Control A on Windows, or click and drag to select everything. Then go to the Export panel on the right side. Set the format to PNG, not JPG. Set the scale to 1x or 2x. The assignment requires the file to be between 5 and 25 megabytes. Too small and you may under-deliver the detail the rubric expects. Too large and Canvas may reject the upload. Before you export, rename the output. Figma defaults to the frame name, which is probably not what you want. Rename it first — to exactly LastName_FirstName_Week2.png — and then export. Do not save a file called 'Figma Export (1).png' and try to rename it afterward in the file system. Rename it in Figma before the export runs. That is the order.

artifactLines: 1. Select all frames — Cmd A (Mac) / Ctrl A (Windows), or click-drag to select all
2. Export panel → Format: PNG  (not JPG)
3. Scale: 1× or 2×  |  Target size: 5–25 MB
4. Rename BEFORE exporting — Figma uses the frame name by default
5. Export as: LastName_FirstName_Week2.png
shot.sparkLine: select all → PNG → size check → rename first → export

beats.narration_text: The filename. It is LastName_FirstName_Week2.png, and it has to be exactly that. Three things to verify. First: last name comes before first name — not the other way around. Second: underscores between each part, not spaces, not dashes. A space in a filename causes problems in some upload systems and is almost certainly not what the rubric expects. Third: Week2, not Week_2, not week2 in lowercase, not Assignment2. The exact string is Week2 — capital W, no underscore, no space. A wrong filename is a five-point deduction on the screen grab requirement. That is five points for a typo you can prevent right now by reading the filename once before you submit.

artifactLines: LastName_FirstName_Week2.png
Check 1: Last name BEFORE first name
Check 2: Underscores between parts — not spaces, not dashes
Check 3: Week2 — capital W, no underscore, no space  (not week2 / Week_2 / Assignment2)
Wrong filename = -5 deduction on screen grab requirement
shot.sparkLine: LastName_FirstName_Week2.png — read it once, check all three

beats.narration_text: One honesty note before you submit. The assignment brief has a deadline conflict. The header of the document says May 22, 2026. The submission block inside the document says January 23, 2026. Those two dates cannot both be correct. I am not going to guess which one is right — that is a factual question about your course, and I am not enrolled in it. Check the course Canvas page directly, or send your instructor a quick message and ask. Do not assume either date is correct. Submit against the confirmed date. This takes two minutes to verify and prevents a potential zero for a late submission.

artifactLines: The assignment brief has a conflict:
· Header says: May 22, 2026
· Submission block says: January 23, 2026
These cannot both be correct.
→ Check Canvas or ask your instructor — do not guess.
→ Submit against the confirmed date only.
shot.sparkLine: conflicting dates → check Canvas → confirm → submit

beats.narration_text: Submitting in Canvas. Two items go in. First, the Figma link. Paste it into the text field or the URL field — whichever Canvas shows you for this assignment. Make sure you are pasting the 'view and comment' share link, not the editor link. The editor link requires a Figma account to open. The share link does not. Second, attach the PNG file. Use the file attachment button in Canvas, select LastName_FirstName_Week2.png, and watch the upload complete before you click Submit. Do not click Submit while the file is still uploading. Submit once. If you need to resubmit, check your course's Canvas policy — some assignments lock after the deadline, and some allow one resubmission. Know the policy before you need it.

artifactLines: Item 1 — Figma link:
· Paste the 'view and comment' share link — NOT the editor link
· Editor link requires a Figma account; share link does not
Item 2 — PNG file:
· Attach LastName_FirstName_Week2.png via the file attachment button
· Wait for upload to complete before clicking Submit
Submit once — check course policy before attempting a resubmission
shot.sparkLine: share link → attach PNG → upload complete → submit once

beats.narration_text: The deduction list from the rubric, read on camera. Minus five for not submitting on Figma — meaning you submitted only a static file with no shareable link. Minus ten for unrealistic scope — more than one agent as the MVP, or a project that could not be built in a single week. Minus fifteen for no Madison integration — agents that are not grounded in real Madison layers or components. Minus five for the missing screen grab — you sent only the link and did not attach the file. That is a total of 35 possible points you can lose from a 100-point assignment, and every one of those deductions is avoidable right now, before you click Submit. The rubric is not punishing complexity. It is punishing skipped steps.

artifactLines: -5   Not on Figma — submitted only a static file, no shareable link
-10  Unrealistic scope — more than one agent as MVP, or not buildable in one week
-15  No Madison integration — agents not grounded in real Madison layers/components
-5   Missing screen grab — only the link submitted, no file attached
Total: 35 points avoidable before you click Submit

beats.narration_text: The submission rules. Figma link: view and comment access, verified in incognito before submitting. Screen grab: PNG, 5 to 25 megabytes, named exactly LastName_FirstName_Week2.png — last name first, underscores, capital W, no underscore between Week and 2. Canvas submission: both items, link and file, file upload confirmed before clicking Submit. Deadline: check Canvas or ask your instructor — the brief has a conflict between May 22 and January 23. The rubric deducts up to 35 points for submission errors that have nothing to do with the quality of your PRD. Do not let that happen.

artifactLines: Figma link: view + comment access · verified in incognito
Screen grab: PNG · 5–25 MB · LastName_FirstName_Week2.png (exact)
· Last name first · underscores · Week2 (capital W, no underscore)
Canvas: link + file · wait for upload · submit once
Deadline: confirm with instructor (brief conflict: May 22 vs Jan 23)
Rubric deducts up to 35 pts for avoidable submission errors — check all before Submit

beats.narration_text: Your turn — and I mean this literally, before you submit. Share your board link with one classmate and ask them three questions using this prompt. If they can't answer all three clearly, that is your revision list before the board goes in. The questions are designed to surface the exact things the rubric is checking: can the scope be understood from the board alone, is the week-three plan realistic, and do the agents each have a distinct job. A peer who answers yes-yes-yes with a confident explanation is a green light. Anything else is a fix you still have time to make.

shot.topic: Peer review your board before submitting
shot.segment: Share with a classmate — ask them this
shot.command: Here is the link to my Assignment 2 Figma board: [YOUR LINK]. Review the board and tell me: (1) Can you tell, from the board alone, exactly what one n8n workflow I'm building in Week 3? (2) Is there anything on the board that looks like it would take more than one week to build? (3) Do the three agents each have a clearly different job? For each question: answer yes or no and explain why in one sentence.
shot.runningText: share with a classmate — if they can't answer all three, that's your revision list…
output: Q1: Can they name exactly what one n8n workflow you're building?  yes / no
Q2: Does anything look like it takes more than one week?  yes / no
Q3: Do all three agents have clearly different jobs?  yes / no
Any 'no' = revise that section before submitting

beats.narration_text: Ship It: Figma Link and Screen-Grab Submission.
